coredata.dc:title: High-efficiency luminescent solar concentrators based on Composition-tunable Eco-friendly Core/shell quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: You, Yimin
Tong, Xin
Imran Channa, Ali
Zhi, Huaqian
Cai, Mengke
Zhao, Hongyang
Xia, Li
Liu, Guiju
Zhao, Haiguang
Wang, Zhiming
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) fabricated using colloidal quantum dots (QDs) are considered as promising cost-effective solar collectors for future building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV). Nevertheless, the solar-to-electricity conversion efficiency of the LSCs is commonly low due to limited quantum efficiency and large reabsorption loss of QDs. In addition, most of the efficient LSCs contain heavy metal-based QDs, limiting their potential commercial applications. Herein, we demonstrate a novel type of eco-friendly CuGaAlS/ZnS (CGAS/ZnS) core/shell QDs with efficient surface passivation and manipulated their optical properties by varying the copper contents, which effectively regulates the intrinsic band structure and radiative recombination dynamics. As a result, the optimal Cu-deficient CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs exhibit the highest photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of ∼ 91 % and large Stokes shift (∼0.81 eV). The prepared QDs were applied as emitters and integrated in polymer matrix to fabricate LSCs (10 × 10 × 0.15 cm3), delivering an unprecedented power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 4.29 % under standard one sun AM 1.5G illumination (100 mW cm−2). Our results imply that tuning the components of multinary environment-friendly core/shell QDs endows the feasibility to realize low-cost and high-efficiency BIPV applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Environment-friendly
Colloidal quantum dots
Core/shell structure
Tunable optical properties
Luminescent solar concentrators

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of the CGAS/ZnS (a-d) #1–4 core/shell QDs with inset HRTEM images and size distributions. (e) XRD patterns of the CGAS/ZnS #1–5 core/shell QDs. (f) HAADF-STEM images of the CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs and (g-k) relevant EDS mapping of all the elements including Cu, Al, Ga, Zn and S. (l) XPS survey spectrum of the CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs and corresponding HRXPS (m) Zn 2p and (n) S 2p spectra.

(a) Normalized UV–vis absorption spectra of the CGAS/ZnS #1–5 core/shell QDs in toluene with inset photographs under UV light illumination. (b) Band gap values obtained from Tauc plots. (c) PL spectra of the CGAS/ZnS #1–4 core/shell QDs. (d) Schematic diagram of radiative combination pathways of CB-to-VCu and DAP transition. (e) Transient PL decay curves of the CGAS/ZnS #1–4 core/shell QDs. (f) Photo-stability test (10 h) of the CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs upon UV light irradiation (405 nm, 2 mW/cm2).

(a) Working mechanism of CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs-based LSC under sunlight illumination. (b) Absorption and (c) transparency spectra of CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs-based LSC. (d) Schematic diagram showing output PL intensity of LSC under different optical paths. (e) PL spectra and (f) PL peak intensity of the QDs-LSC detected under different optical distances. (g) PL spectra and (h) integrated PL intensity of as-fabricated CGAS/ZnS #4 QDs-LSC at different temperatures.

Schematic diagrams of (a) a BIPV window consisting of (b) LSCs based on (c) CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs with attached PV cells. (d) Photographs and (e) corresponding J–V curves of CGAS/ZnS #4 QDs-LSCs (dimensions: 10 × 10 × 0.15 cm3) under standard one sun AM 1.5G illumination (100 mW cm−2). (f) Photographs and (g) corresponding J–V curves of CGAS/ZnS #4 QDs-LSCs (dimensions: 20 × 20 × 0.15 cm3) under natural light (45 mW cm−2) illumination. (h) Photographs and (i) relevant J–V curves of CGAS/ZnS #1 QDs-LSCs (dimensions: 10 × 10 × 0.15 cm3) under AM 1.5G illumination (90 mW cm−2).

head.ce:title: High-efficiency luminescent solar concentrators based on Composition-tunable Eco-friendly Core/shell quantum dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Fundamental and Frontier Sciences
University of Electronic Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) fabricated using colloidal quantum dots (QDs) are considered as promising cost-effective solar collectors for future building-integrated photovoltaics (BIPV). Nevertheless, the solar-to-electricity conversion efficiency of the LSCs is commonly low due to limited quantum efficiency and large reabsorption loss of QDs. In addition, most of the efficient LSCs contain heavy metal-based QDs, limiting their potential commercial applications. Herein, we demonstrate a novel type of eco-friendly CuGaAlS/ZnS (CGAS/ZnS) core/shell QDs with efficient surface passivation and manipulated their optical properties by varying the copper contents, which effectively regulates the intrinsic band structure and radiative recombination dynamics. As a result, the optimal Cu-deficient CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs exhibit the highest photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY) of ∼ 91 % and large Stokes shift (∼0.81 eV). The prepared QDs were applied as emitters and integrated in polymer matrix to fabricate LSCs (10 × 10 × 0.15 cm3), delivering an unprecedented power conversion efficiency (PCE) of 4.29 % under standard one sun AM 1.5G illumination (100 mW cm−2). Our results imply that tuning the components of multinary environment-friendly core/shell QDs endows the feasibility to realize low-cost and high-efficiency BIPV applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, the massive consumption of conventional fossil fuels has caused significant environment issues (e.g., air pollution, global warming) and worldwide energy shortages [1]. Solar energy, as an earth-abundant and sustainable energy resource, has been extensively exploited and utilized in the last several decades [2,3], triggering the rapidly developed photovoltaic (PV) technologies (e.g., solar cell) that enables the conversion of sunlight into electricity [4–8]. However, it is still challenging to effectively collect and concentrate the sunlight in a facile manner, which enables the cost reduction of conventional PV devices via decreasing the active area used to generate identical amount of electricity [9]. Luminescent solar concentrators (LSCs) are large-scale sunlight collection/concentration devices comprised of an optical waveguide slab with embedded luminescent species and integrated PV cells, which have been applied to optimize the traditional PV systems since 1976 [10]. In a typical device operation process, the embedded luminophores in the waveguide can absorb the incident sunlight and re-emit the photons that are guided to the smaller edges of LSCs by total internal reflection, where the attached PV cells can generate photocurrent under concentrated light illumination [11–13]. In this case, once the power conversion efficiency (PCE) of the LSC is over 6 % [14], they are able to reduce the cost of PV systems via replacing the solar cells and hold great promise in building-integrated PV (BIPV) due to flexible design of their device parameters including the color, weight, geometry and transparency etc [15]. In general, it is the key to select luminescent species showing high photoluminescence quantum yield (PLQY), large Stokes shift (small re-absorption) and long-term photo/chemical stability to achieve highly efficient LSCs [11,16,17].

To date, various kinds of luminescent species including organic dyes/polymers, perovskite nanocrystals, and colloidal quantum dots (QDs) have been employed to fabricate highly efficient LSCs [18–21]. However, the organic dyes/polymers and perovskite nanocrystals generally suffer from high reabsorption and poor photo-stability under light illumination [17,22]. In contrast, colloidal QDs have become promising luminescent species in LSC applications due to their high absorption coefficient, large Stokes shift, tunable absorption/emission, high PLQY and long-term photo/chemical stability. Most of the QDs-based LSCs developed in earlier research usually consist of heavy metal-based QDs (e.g., PbS/Se and CdS/Se QDs), which are harmful to the natural environment and human health [14,23,24]. Recently, a series of eco-friendly QDs have been proposed and applied as luminescent species in LSCs, examples include I-III-VI QDs, silicon QDs and carbon QDs, etc. [14,25–27]. In particular, multinary I-III-VI QDs with facile composition-tunable optical properties and intrinsic large Stokes shift have demonstrated great potential in various optoelectronic devices [28–33]. Notably, the abundant surface defects of I-III-VI QDs can also be effectively passivated by the growth of appropriate wide-band-gap shell materials (e.g., ZnS), resulting in core/shell structured QDs with largely enhanced PLQY that are beneficial to the luminescent applications including LSCs.

Among all kinds of I-III-VI core/shell QDs, the CuGaS2/ZnS (CGS/ZnS) core/shell QDs with high PLQY, large Stokes shift, small self-absorption loss, and long-term photo-stability are considered as promising building blocks for PV systems including light-emitting diodes (LEDs) and LSCs [34–36]. However, the expensive Ga precursor is necessary for the successful preparation of CGS/ZnS QDs, which impedes their cost-effective and large-scale production for practical applications and commercial perspectives [35]. In comparison, another attractive I-III-VI CuAlS2/ZnS (CAS/ZnS) core/shell QDs synthesized using low-cost and earth-abundant Al precursors can help to reduce the overall cost for QD’s synthesis. Nevertheless, to date, the reported CAS/ZnS core/shell QDs have very low PLQY (∼20 %), which hinders their potential applications in various of optoelectronic devices [37,38]. Obviously, CGS/ZnS and CAS/ZnS core/shell QDs are complementary and may be combined and tailored to achieve both lower synthesis cost and improved optical properties, which is similar to other typical I-III-VI QD systems including CuAlxFe1−xS2 and CuInxGa1−xS2 QDs [39,40]. Notably, controllable donor or acceptor states can be realized by varying the Cu contents in these multinary I-III-VI QDs, which potentially enables the manipulation of the radiative recombination dynamics inside QDs for largely boosted PLQY [36,41,42].

In this work, we demonstrated a new type of eco-friendly CuxGayAlyS2 (CGAS)/ZnS core/shell QDs (x = 6, 4, 2, 1, 0.5 and y = 4) and manipulated their optical properties via tuning the composition of core materials. It is found that increasing the Cu contents can lead to red-shifted absorption/emission due to the narrowed core QD’s band gap. In contrast, with decreasing Cu contents, the obtained Cu-deficient CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs exhibit largely enhanced PLQY, which is ascribed to both the sufficient ZnS shell surface passivation and improved Cu defects-induced radiative recombination. A remarkable PLQY of 91 % (in toluene) is achieved for the CGAS/ZnS QDs with Cu/Ga/Al molar ratios of 1/4/4, which were then applied to fabricate green-emitting LSCs (10 × 10 × 0.15 cm3), showing superior performance with a PCE of 4.29 % under standard one sun illumination (AM 1.5G, 100 mW cm−2). Besides, the fabricated LSCs with large-area (20 × 20 × 0.15 cm3) still exhibited a considerable PCE of 0.55 % under natural sunlight illumination (45 mW cm−2). Our finding highlights that these cost-effective and eco-friendly CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs with composition-dependent optical properties hold great promise for the developments of future practical BIPV technologies and other optoelectronic devices.

Eco-friendly CGAS core and CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs were synthesized based on a previously reported one-pot method with some modifications (details are described in Supporting Information and Scheme S1) [35]. Fig. S1a shows a typical transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of CGAS core QDs, exhibiting a quasi-spherical morphology with mean size of ∼ 2 nm and a lattice spacing of ∼ 3.06 Å, which lies between the lattice spacings of 3.04 nm Å [(112) plane of pure CuGaS2] and 3.07 Å [(112) plane of pure CuAlS2] that can be indexed the (112) plane of alloyed CGAS. Meanwhile, the selected area electron diffraction (SAED) and X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of CGAS core QDs (Fig. S1b, c) display-three characteristic rings/peaks between the (112), (204) and (312) planes of pure CuGaS2 (JCPDS No. 25–0279) and CuAlS2 (JCPDS No. 25–0014) with tetragonal crystal structure, thus further demonstrating the successful synthesis of alloyed CGAS core [35]. After ZnS-shell growth, the as-prepared CuxGayAlyS2/ZnS core/shell QDs (x = 6, 4, 2, 1, 0.5 and y = 4) with different Cu contents were labeled as CGAS/ZnS #1–5, respectively, as summarized in Table S1. In comparison to the CGAS bare QDs, the TEM images of the CGAS/ZnS #1–5 core/shell QDs (Fig. 1 a–d and Figure S2) still exhibit similar quasi-spherical morphology, while the sizes of core/shell QDs changed to be larger (∼3.6–3.9 nm) than the core QD size (∼2 nm) due to ZnS shell over-coating. All the lattice spacings of core/shell QD samples in high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) images (insets of Fig. 1a–d) were measured to be ∼ 3.12 Å, which is in accordance with the (111) plane of zinc-blende ZnS (JCPDS No. 65-0309). Besides, the SAED and XRD patterns (Fig. 1e and Figure S3) of CGAS/ZnS #1–5 QDs also depict characteristic rings/peaks that agree well with the (111), (220) and (311) planes of the zinc-blende ZnS, indicating the realization of ZnS shell coating on CGAS core, which is consistent with the dominant crystalline phase of shell materials in other core/shell QD systems [43,44].

As shown in Fig. 1f–k, the high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) images, elemental mapping and energy dispersive spectrometer (EDS) spectrum (Figure S4) of as-synthesized CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs can verify the presence of all elements (Cu, Ga, Al, S and Zn) contained in the QDs. As listed in Table S2, inductively coupled plasma optical emission spectrometry (ICP-OES) measurements further affirmed the existence of Cu, Ga, Al, S and Zn elements in the CGAS/ZnS QDs, in which the contents of Zn and S elements in the shell are much higher than that of the Cu, Ga, Al elements in the core, suggesting the growth of relatively thick ZnS shell on CGAS core QDs for effective surface passivation. The chemical compositions of CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs were also probed by using X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), Fig. 1l shows the XPS survey spectrum of CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs, wherein the XPS signals of Zn 2p and S 2p are much stronger than that of the Cu 2p, Ga 3d and Al 2p owing to the formation of thick ZnS shell on pristine CGAS core [45]. Specifically, Figure S5 exhibits the high-resolution XPS (HRXPS) spectra of Cu, Ga and Al elements in core with detected binding energies of 952.4/933.0 eV, 19.1 eV, and 75.3 eV that are assigned to Cu 2p, Ga 3d and Al 2p, respectively. As for the Zn and S elements in ZnS shell, we detected stronger XPS signals of Zn 2p (Fig. 1m) at binding energies of 1045.9/1022.7 eV. Especially, in addition to the XPS signals of S 2p with binding energies of 162.6/161.4 eV, there are the other two components at binding energies of 164.2/163 eV (Fig. 1n), which can be assigned to the attached thiol molecules on CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs [46,47]. These excess sulfur contents were originated from the usage of 1-dodecanethiol during QD’s synthesis. All the results verify the growth of thick ZnS shell materials on pristine CGAS core.

The composition-dependent optical properties of QDs were measured and displayed in Fig. 2 . Fig. 2a depicts the normalized absorption spectra of CGAS/ZnS #1–5 core/shell QDs, wherein a gradually blue-shifted absorbance from #1 to #5 samples with decreasing Cu contents (Cu/Ga/Al ratios from 6/4/4 to 0.5/4/4) can be observed, which is derived from the enlarged band gap of QDs with less Cu incorporation, since the bulk band gap values of Ga2S3 (∼3.3 eV) and Al2S3 (3.48 eV) are comparatively larger than Cu2S (∼1.2 eV) [48,49]. On the basis of the absorption spectra, the estimated band gap values obtained from Tauc plots (Figure S7) of CGAS/ZnS #1–5 QDs are ∼ 3.15, 3.31, 3.35, 3.38 and 3.43 eV, respectively, as shown in Fig. 2b. Consistently, the normalized PL spectra of the CGAS/ZnS QDs also exhibit blue-shifted PL peaks from 578 nm (#1) to 543 nm (#2), 525 nm (#3), 495 nm (#4) and 478 nm (#5), as displayed in Fig. 2c and Figure S8.

The sufficient passivation of CGAS core QD’s surface defect states by ZnS shell enables the improved optical properties, specifically, the formed type I electronic band structure between CGAS core and ZnS shell can effectively confine both the electrons and holes into the core region for enhanced exciton radiative recombination [30]. We further fitted the PL spectra and conducted PLQY and transient PL measurements to further study the optical properties of CGAS/ZnS QDs. As exhibited in Figure S9, the PL spectra of core/shell QDs were fitted by using Gaussian function to reveal the composition-dependent emission mechanism, in which two peaks with high and low energy emission are referred to Peak 1 and Peak 2, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 2d, the radiative recombination of electrons in conduction band (CB) and holes located at Cu vacancy (VCu), known as CB-to-VCu transition, is the origin of Peak 1 with high energy emission, which is consistent with other similar I-III-VI group core/shell QD systems [36,41,50–52]. In parallel, the intrinsic Cu-related GaCu, AlCu or ZnCu defects are responsible for the Peak 2 with low energy emission [36]. These Cu-related defects can be formed during the core formation and ZnS shelling processes and act as donor levels to radiatively recombine with VCu acceptor levels, which is defined as donor–acceptor pair (DAP) recombination. The concentration of donor and acceptor recombination centers can significantly determine the luminescence properties (i.e., PLQY) of I-III-VI QDs [41,42,53]. Therefore, the Cu-deficient QDs are desirable to provide more VCu acceptor states for both improved radiative CB-to-VCu and DAP recombination, thus leading to highly boosted PLQY. In our case, the ratios of Peak 2/(Peak 1 + Peak 2) increased with decreasing Cu contents (Figure S9f), which is indicative of the increasing radiative recombination channels in the Cu-deficient CGAS/ZnS QDs that gives rise to improved PLQY [36,54].

Consequently, as shown in Table S3 and Figure S10, the measured PLQYs of the CGAS/ZnS #1–5 QDs increase with less Cu contents (more donor and acceptor recombination centers), wherein the CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs with bright green emission show a remarkable PLQY of ∼ 91 %. However, the PLQY of CGAS/ZnS #5 QDs (88 %) with the lowest Cu content is slightly lower than that of the CGAS/ZnS #4 QDs (91 %), which is probably due to the emerging non-radiative recombination such as Auger recombination [54]. Fig. 2e and Figure S11 show the transient PL decay curves of QDs, in which the average PL lifetimes of CGAS/ZnS #1–5 samples were estimated to be ∼ 512, 829, 961, 1177 and 423 ns (Fitting details tabulated in Table S4), respectively. Particularly, the CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs shows the longest PL lifetime of around 1.2 μs, which indicates the sufficient surface defects passivation of CGAS core via ZnS shell coating, as also evidenced by the highest PLQY (∼91 %). The photo-stability of the optimized CGAS/ZnS #4 QDs is further evaluated under UV light illumination (405 nm, 2 mW/cm2) and exhibited in Fig. 2f, showing an impressive photo-stability that retains 93 % of integrated PL intensity (Figure S12) even after 10 h UV light illumination. Moreover, a large Stokes shift of 0.81 eV (Figure S13) is obtained for CGAS/ZnS #4 QDs and the calculated synthetic cost is 21 % lower than that of the CGS/ZnS QDs under identical conditions (Table S5). Overall, the cost-effectiveness, high photo-stability, large Stokes shift and high PLQY of the CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs demonstrate their great potential in long-term stable and high-efficiency LSCs applications.

Afterwards, we incorporated the core/shell QDs into poly(lauryl methacrylate-co-ethylene glycol dimethacrylate) (PLMA-co-EGDA) polymer matrix to fabricate the LSC devices (details can be found in Supporting Information). A brief scheme of working mechanism of the QD-LSC is illustrated in Fig. 3 a, wherein the QDs absorb the incident sunlight through the top surface of an LSC to emit the light, which is then guided to the smaller area of the LSC attached with PV cells via total internal reflection [11,55]. The optical absorption and transparency spectra of LSCs were measured and shown in Fig. 3b, c, displaying similar absorption feature with the CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs dispersed in toluene and excellent transparency. The optical distance-dependent PL intensity of the CGAS/ZnS #4 QD-LSC was studied and relevant schematic graph is exhibited in Fig. 3d. As displayed in Fig. 3e, f, the PL intensity of device gradually reduced with prolonged optical distance, which is due to the scattering loss during the transportation of emitted light from QDs. Besides, there is no obvious red-shift or peak width narrowing in optical distance-dependent PL spectra (Figure S14), thus indicating a very small reabsorption loss from the CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs [56,57].

The fabricated QD-LSCs are supposed to be applied in ambient atmosphere for operation in outdoor conditions under various temperature, therefore, the thermal stability plays a significant role in QDs-LSCs system. As shown in Fig. 3g, h, the thermal effect of the prepared QD-LSCs was studied by testing the PL spectra of LSC device under different temperature, wherein the integrated PL intensity reduces with increasing temperature and shows no significant PL peak shift or shape variation. However, the integrated PL intensity can maintain 74 % of its original value at relatively high temperature of 40 ℃. Even at extremely high temperature of 80 ℃, the device can still retain 50 % of its original integrated PL intensity. All the results demonstrate the outstanding thermal stability of as-fabricated CGAS/ZnS #4 QDs-LSCs and prove their potential practical applications in complex ambient atmosphere.

The schematic diagrams for a BIPV window consisting of CGAS/ZnS #4 QDs-based LSCs are exhibited in Fig. 4 a–c, and the PCEs of these QD-LSCs were tested by using a calibrated silicon PV cell. Fig. 4d shows the photograph of CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs-based LSC with dimensions of 10 × 10 × 0.15 cm3 under standard 1 sun illumination (AM 1.5G, 100 mW/cm2), exhibiting a high Jsc of 11.49 mA cm2 and a remarkable PCE of 4.29 % (Fig. 4e). Such unprecedented PCE value is mainly originated from the QDs’ high PLQY and considerably large Stokes shift, which is comparable to other types of QDs-based LSCs with various dimensions, as summarized in Table S6 and Figure S15. As the incident light intensity and illuminated area of LSCs have a significant impact on the device performance, we fabricated a large-area LSC based on CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs with dimensions of 20 × 20 × 0.15 cm3 and tested the performance under simulated natural sunlight (45 mW/cm2), as shown in Fig. 4f. Consequently, the prepared large-area QDs-LSC (400 cm2) still exhibited a PCE as high as 0.54 % (Fig. 4g) under low-intensity natural sunlight, which shows great potential in future real-life BIPV applications.

Moreover, to prove the potential of other CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs (e.g., CGAS/ZnS #1 core/shell QDs with a relatively low PLQY of 11 %) in LSCs application, we fabricated corresponding QDs-LSC with dimensions of 10 × 10 × 0.15 cm3 (Fig. 4h), which exhibited a PCE of 0.77 % (Fig. 4i) under AM 1.5G illumination (90 mW cm−2). Although the obtained PCE of CGAS/ZnS #1 core/shell QDs-LSC is lower than that of the optimized CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs-based device due to limited PLQY, the emitted orange light at longer wavelength from CGAS/ZnS #1 core/shell QDs-LSC is promising for rationally designed tandem CGAS/ZnS core/shell QD-LSCs to pursue higher device performance.

In summary, a new type of environment-friendly CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs with composition-tunable optical properties were successfully synthesized for LSC application. By increasing the Cu contents in the core, as-prepared CGAS/ZnS core/shell QDs exhibit red-shifted absorption and emission spectra due to band gap narrowing. It is demonstrated that the Cu-deficient CGAS/ZnS QDs are able to exhibit the highest PLQY due to both efficient ZnS shell passivation and enhanced radiative DAP recombination. Particularly, the optimized CGAS/ZnS #4 core/shell QDs with green emission, high PLQY (∼91 %) and a large Stokes shift (∼0.81 eV) were used to fabricate LSCs with typical dimensions of 10 × 10 × 0.15 cm3, delivering a remarkable PCE of 4.29 % under standard 1 sun illumination (AM 1.5G, 100 mW cm−2). Meanwhile, as-fabricated LSCs showed excellent thermal stability that maintains 73 % of the integrated PL intensity under relatively high temperature of 40 ℃. For the QD-LSCs with larger dimensions of 20 × 20 × 0.15 cm3, a comparable PCE of 0.54 % can be obtained under natural light illumination (45 mW cm−2). Furthermore, the LSC device based on the CGAS/ZnS #1 core/shell QDs with orange emission (10 × 10 × 0.15 cm3) still exhibited a PCE of 0.77 % under AM 1.5G illumination (90 mW cm−2). The results imply that optimizing the composition of eco-friendly I-III-VI group core/shell QDs is promising to tailor the optical properties (PLQY, Stokes shift etc.) for realization of cost-effective, high-performance, large-area and eco-friendly BIPV applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: X.T. acknowledges the support from National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2019YFE0121600), National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 22105031 and 62011530131) and Sichuan Science and Technology Program (No. 2021YFH0054). Z.M.W. is grateful to the National Key Research and Development Program of China (No. 2019YFB2203400) and the “111 Project” (No. B20030).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2022.139490.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Unextractable fossil fuels in a 1.5 °C world

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Welsby, J. Price, S. Pye, P. Ekins, Unextractable fossil fuels in a 1.5 °C world, Nature 597 (2021) 230-234.
P. Tao, G. Ni, C. Song, W. Shang, J. Wu, J. Zhu, G. Chen, T. Deng, Solar-driven interfacial evaporation, Nat. Energy. 3 (2018) 1031-1041.
J. Gong, C. Li, M.R. Wasielewski, Advances in solar energy conversion, Chem. So. Rev. 48 (2019) 1862-1864.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Emerging perovskite solar cell technology: Remedial actions for the foremost challenges
